result:
The old woman at the farmer's market sells honey in mason jars with handwritten labels, and every Saturday morning she tells me the same story about her bees. How they know her footsteps. How they dance figure-eights to share directions to the best wildflower patches. How sometimes, on summer evenings, she sits beside the hives and hums Bach inventions, convinced they work harder when the music is mathematical.

I don't know if any of it's true, but I buy three jars every week.

Truth is a funny thing anyway. Yesterday, my daughter asked me if clouds have feelings, and I said yes without thinking. Now she waves at them from the car window, shouting encouragement at the particularly gray ones. "You're doing great!" she yells at a storm front. Who am I to say she's wrong? Maybe the universe needs more unfounded kindness.

I've been thinking about time lately—how it pooled like honey when I was seven, thick and golden, every summer day an eternity of grass stains and sidewalk chalk. Now it pours through my fingers like water. My father calls less often these days, and when he does, he repeats stories I've heard before. Each time, I act surprised at the punchline. This is love, I think: pretending his jokes are new.

There's a particular quality of light that only exists at 4 PM in October, slanted and nostalgic, making everything look like a memory even as it's happening. I tried to photograph it once, but the camera couldn't capture what my eyes knew—that specific amber weight, how it made the ordinary world feel like it was saying goodbye.

My neighbor grows tomatoes that taste like the 1980s. He saves seeds from his grandmother's plants, year after year, a genetic line of flavor stretching back to before I was born. When he gives me a bag of Sun Golds, warm from the vine, I eat them like candy and think about inheritance—what we pass down besides eyes and bone structure. The way my mother held a pencil. My grandfather's laugh. This peculiar melancholy that hits every Sunday evening.

Sometimes I walk through the city at dawn, before the coffee shops open, when the streets belong to pigeons and delivery trucks. There's something holy about a sleeping city, all that human complexity temporarily paused. The buildings look surprised to be witnessed without their usual crowd of witnesses. I pick up pieces of other people's lives: a dropped grocery list (eggs, birthday candles, cat food, hope), a child's mitten, a love letter torn in half (I only find one piece, which seems right somehow).

The barista at my usual café has a tattoo of a whale on her forearm. Tuesday, she told me whales have regional dialects, that families develop their own subtle variations in their calls. I wonder if this is true for humans too—if there's a particular way my family says "I love you" that sounds different from yours. The way we all go quiet during the second verse of certain songs. How we save twist ties in a drawer that's meant for something else.

Memory is unreliable, which might be its best feature. I'm certain my grandmother's kitchen was yellow, but my sister swears it was blue. We're probably both wrong, but we're building something between us with these misremembered walls—a shared mythology of a woman who made soup from stones and stories, who could cure hiccups by surprise and always knew when it would rain.

Last week, I found a note I'd written to myself years ago: "Remember the foxes." I have no idea what foxes I meant. Urban foxes at midnight? A nature documentary? Some metaphorical foxes I thought were important? But now I think about foxes daily—their sharp faces, their implausible red, how they move through the world like smoke with teeth. Maybe forgetting the specific is how we remember the essential.

The couple upstairs fights in a language I don't understand, but I recognize the rhythm of reconciliation—the long pause, the soft laughter, footsteps meeting in the middle of the room. Under my window, teenagers practice being in love, all dramatic gestures and borrowed words. They think they invented longing. I want to tell them they're right.

There's so much I want to tell everyone: That the moon follows you home because it loves you. That somewhere, right now, a nurse is holding a stranger's hand. That gardens remember who planted them. That you can grow new neural pathways at any age—your brain literally reshaping itself because you decided to learn harmonica or forgive your brother or stop biting your nails.

In the park, an old man feeds breadcrumbs to birds, even though the signs say not to. "I'm ninety-three," he tells me. "What are they going to do?" The birds know his schedule, gathering before he arrives, a kind of faith. He knows each one—the pigeon with the limp, the crow who likes crusts, the sparrows who wait for him to look away before approaching.

This morning, I woke to find it had rained in the night. The world smelled like beginnings. My daughter put on her boots and went looking for worms to rescue from puddles. She names each one before placing it safely in the grass: Fernando, Stephanie, Doctor Worm, Bob.

We're all just trying to save something, aren't we?

The honey woman's story changes slightly each week. Sometimes the bees know Mozart, sometimes Beethoven. Sometimes they recognize her by her perfume, sometimes by her heartbeat. I've decided all versions are true. In this uncertain world, why not believe in everything?
